nativeRange.habitat: Clear outer reef flats and seaward reefs; generally found at depths of 1–15 m

diet.notes: Primarily grazes macroalgae and microalgae; benefits from regular offerings of dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week. Has been documented accepting high-quality flake and pellet foods as well as protein-based frozen foods such as mysis and krill alongside other tank inhabitants.

imageCandidates.notes: Erik Schlogl, 1800x1289. Sharp full-frame adult showing diagnostic freckled/spotted face, yellow overtones, grey body, dark caudal fin and the dark ear spot behind the eye.
Debra Baker (via iNaturalist/GBIF), 1676x1410. Crisp darker adult with clear white/yellow freckle spotting on the face and dark ear spot; blue in-situ cast.
Izuzuki, 640x480. Uniformly dark adult form showing facial spotting and pale caudal-peduncle band; lower resolution, kept for color-form variety.

summary: The Black-Eared Tang is a large, robust Indo-Pacific surgeonfish appreciated for its distinctive freckled face and efficient algae-grazing. Generally considered best kept singly in spacious reef or FOWLR systems, it tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly shaped surgeonfish.
careNotes:
A large, established tank of at least around 1,000 liters is generally advised to give this active swimmer adequate territory. Temperature is commonly recommended around 23–26°C, though some sources suggest ranges reaching as low as 22°C or as high as 28°C.

Water quality should be stable and well-oxygenated; this species is reported to acclimate well to standard reef parameters (SG ~1.022–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12).

Diet should lean heavily toward marine algae; dried seaweed and spirulina-based foods are advisable at minimum three times weekly. Protein foods (mysis, krill, enriched frozen preparations) can supplement the diet in mixed-community feedings.

The species tends to be territorial toward other Acanthurus tangs and surgeonfish of similar body shape. Adding multiple tang species simultaneously rather than sequentially is generally advised to reduce aggression. It has been known to coexist peaceably with most non-surgeonfish reef inhabitants.

Like all acanthurids, this species possesses a sharp caudal spine (the 'scalpel') used for defense; it is not venomous but can inflict lacerations. Handle with care during transfers.

Juveniles display beige bodies with pale yellow spots; adults develop a smoky brown coloration with yellow overtones, prominent freckle-like spotting on the head and forehead, and in some individuals the caudal fin may become predominantly white. Large adults develop a noticeably convex head profile.

Protogyny has not been well documented for this species in available sources; sex reversal is left as unknown. Captive breeding has not been reported.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Black-Eared Tang has not been documented. In the wild, Acanthurus surgeonfish are broadcast spawners, releasing eggs and sperm freely into the water column during group spawning events. Reproduction in aquaria is considered highly unlikely given the very large tank volumes this species requires and the absence of any reported successes in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Black-Eared Tang. Provided adult size (16 in), tank minimum (300 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet, and temperament.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (40 cm), native range, temperature (24–28°C), taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), and IUCN status (Least Concern). Confirmed species identity as Acanthurus maculiceps.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (40 cm), minimum tank (1500 L), SG (~1.026), reef-safe status, native range, and diet. Tank volume of 1500 L is notably higher than other sources; included in average.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), semi-aggressive temperament, herbivorous diet, and reef compatibility. Adult size listed as 13 in (33 cm) — lower than other sources; included in average. Minimum tank listed as 125 gal (473 L).

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been recorded. In the wild the species is a broadcast spawner; the tank volumes and conditions required for natural spawning behavior are not realistically achievable in home aquaria.
